vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Authentication Retry Prompts Permitted Per-Session in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
vulnerability.description: To configure the number of retry prompts that are permitted per-session: Edit the /etc/security/pwquality.conf to include retry= , or a lower value if site policy is more restrictive. The profile requirement is a maximum of retry= prompts per session.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_password_pam_retry='' if grep -sq retry /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/retry/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^retry") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_retry" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m 1 -i -e "^retry\\>" "/etc/security/pwqual

triage_decision.reason: This is a standard PAM configuration change in /etc/security/pwquality.conf that limits the number of password change retries. It follows standard STIG hardening patterns for password policy and does not impact system availability or existing user access.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Minor risk of user frustration if retry limits are set too low during password changes

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan is sound and directly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the 'retry' parameter is set in the appropriate configuration file. Checking the .d/ directory first is a good practice to prevent conflicting configurations.
concerns: The plan does not explicitly specify how to handle existing values in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ files, though it mentions checking them. If a configuration exists in a .d/ file, it might override the main config file depending on how PAM is ordered.
suggested_improvements: Ensure that if a setting is found in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, it is either updated or the main config change is verified to take precedence according to the system's PAM configuration.
Use a specific value that aligns with organizational policy rather than a generic '3' if a policy document exists.
